content.full_text:
California
SUPERSEDED
Fair Political
I Practices Commission
May 26,
1993
Carolyn Confer
Assistant City Attorney
3900 Main Street
Riverside,
CA
92522
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A-93-147
Dear Ms.
Confer:
This is in response to your request for advice on behalf of
Riverside City Councilmember Alex Clifford regarding his duties
under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform
Act. /
QUESTION
May Councilmember Clifford participate in discussions with
staff in the city’s development department concerning the
demolition of
a building located on property which he,
as a real
estate broker,
has listed for sale?
CONCLUS ION
Councilmember Clifford may participate in discussions
concerning the demolition of the building since the mere listing
thereof does not create a disqualifying economic interest.
FACTS
Councilmember Clifford is a real estate broker.
2
!
As a
broker,
he has a listing for property located within the City of
Riverside.
The councilmember would like to represent the owner of
1/
Government Code Sections 81000—91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000 et q.
All references to regulations are to Title
2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2!
This information was provided during our April 2,
1993
telephone conversation.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


File No.
A—93—147
Page
Two
the property in discussions with city staff about the owner’s
application for financial assistance for the demolition of a
building located on the property.
The city’s development department has a federally funded
program which provides for city-subsidized demolition of property
that has a significant blighting effect in the surrounding area.
Various financial programs are available whereby the city and the
property owner would each pay a certain portion of the cost of
demolition.
Before funding would be approved, the historic value
of the property would be considered,
and permits and approvals
must be obtained from various city departments.
The property which is the subject of the demolition has been
listed with Councilmember Clifford for two and a half years.
The
listing agreement is a standard-type listing providing that the
broker would receive no payment until the property is sold.
The
property is not in escrow.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making,
participating in,
or using their official position to influence a
governmental decision in which they know or have reason to know
they have a financial interest.
An official has a financial interest in a governmental
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally,
on the official,
or on a member of the official’s immediate
family,
or on:
(a)
Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(c)
Any source of income,
other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status,
aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars
($250)
or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within
12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
Section 87103(a) (c)
Emphasis added.


File No.
A—93—147
Page Three
Consequently,
Councilmember Clifford may not make or
participate in the making,
or use his official position to
influence a governmental decision if the decision will have a
reasonably foreseeable
3
! material financial effect on his business
entity or on his sources of income.
4
!
Regulation 18704.3 provides special rules for determining who
is the source of commission income earned in a given sales
transaction.
Regulation 18704.3 provides:
(C)
The sources of commission income
5
!
in a
specific sale or similar transaction include for
each of the following:
*
**
(2)
A real estate broker:
(A)
The person the broker
represents in the transaction;
(B)
If the broker receives a
commission from a transaction conducted
by an agent working under the broker’s
auspices, the person represented by the
agent;
(C)
Any brokerage business entity
through which the broker conducts
business;
and
(D)
Any person who receives a
finder’s or other referral fee for
referring a party to the transaction to
3/
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are
reasonably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made
depends on the facts of each particular case.
An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
likelihood that it will occur.
Certainty is not required.
However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable.
(In re Thorner
(1975)
1
FPPC Ops.
198.)
Section 82030 provides that the income of an individual also
includes a pro—rata share of any income of any business entity or
trust in which the individual or spouse owns, directly, indirectly
or beneficially,
a 10—percent interest or greater.
5/
“Commission Income” means gross payments received as a result
of services rendered as a broker,
agent,
or other salesperson for
a specific sale or similar transaction.
Commission income is
received when it is paid or credited.


File No.
A—93147
Page Four
the broker,
or who makes a referral
pursuant to a contract with the broker.
(d)
For purposes of determining whether
disqualification is required under the provisions
of Sections 87100 and 87103(c), the full gross
value of any commission income for a specific sale
or similar transaction shall be attributed to each
source of income in that sale or transaction.
In a real estate transaction,
sources of “promised” income
are also economic interests.
Income is “promised” once a sale is
pending (i.e.,
the sale is in escrow)
or the client has multiple
listings with the broker.
(Felts Advice Letter,
No.
A-85—130;
Robbins Advice Letter,
No. A-87-074.)
Accordingly, the single listing is not a source of income to
Councilmember Clifford until such time as a sale is pending.
Thus,
he is not prohibited from making or participating in the
making of decisions affecting the real property,
nor is he
prohibited from using his official position to influence a
decision which may have an effect on the listing thereof.
Councilmember Clifford may have discussions with city staff
concerning the application for demolition funds until such time as
the property is in escrow.
However, regardless of our advice,
you
may want to consider the “appearance” of Councilmember Clifford’s
use of his position in this manner.
6
!
I trust this answers your question adequately.
If we can be
of further assistance, please feel free to contact the
Commission’s Legal Division at
(916)
322—5901.
Sincerely,
Jeff Marschner
B
Jeanette E.
Turv 11
Political Reform Consultant
Legal Division
JM/JET/jt
6/
In Felts, the public official relinquished his listings so
that he would derive no “promised”
income from any sales and would
be free to participate in future decisions.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May Councilmember Clifford participate in discussions with
staff in the city’s development department concerning the
demolition of
a building located on property which he,
as a real
estate broker,
has listed for sale?

CONCLUSION: Councilmember Clifford may participate in discussions
concerning the demolition of the building since the mere listing
thereof does not create a disqualifying economic interest.
